coredata.dc:title: A facile approach towards large-scale synthesis of TiO2 nanoparticles derived from egg shell waste with enhanced UV shielding, nano priming and fingerprint real time object detection through YOLOv8x

coredata.dc:description: This study demonstrates an eco-friendly method for synthesizing TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) utilizing an aqueous extract derived from egg shell (ES) waste. This study examines the use of TiO2 NPs for detecting Level I-III fingerprint (FP) features via the powder dusting method. The NPs enhance the visualization of ridge patterns, minutiae points, and pore structures in latent prints. Results show improved clarity, making this method promising for high-resolution forensic FP analysis. This paper explores the use of YOLOv8x, a cutting-edge object detection model, for FP analysis. YOLOv8x advanced architecture allows for rapid and accurate identification of minutiae points, ridge patterns, and other essential FP features. Its high precision and real-time detection enhance the efficiency of FP recognition, making it applicable to forensic investigations and biometric authentication. Experimental results show that YOLOv8x outperforms traditional methods in both accuracy and processing speed, highlighting its superiority in FP analysis tasks. The PVA@TiO2 films are engineered to offer excellent transparency, exceptional flexibility, and efficient UV radiation blocking properties, which have been rigorously evaluated across the UV-A, UV-B, and UV-C spectra. Blocking experiments revealed that the PVA@TiO2 (16 wt%) film effectively blocked 90.11 % of UV-A radiation, 92.82 % of UV-B radiation and 92.72 % of UV-C radiation. A solution of TiO2 NPs at environmentally relevant concentrations (20–160 mg/mL) is utilized as a nano-priming agent to improve the germination and early seedling growth of horse gram seeds. Notably, root length increased by 61 %, while shoot length grew by 58 %. The vigor index, an essential indicator of seedling health and strength, is significantly greater in the treated seeds, achieving a value of 2460, compared to 945 in the control group. This study presents a multifunctional material designed for its efficacy in FP detection, UV blocking capabilities, and beneficial effects on seed germination, providing a wide range of potential applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study demonstrates an eco-friendly method for synthesizing TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) utilizing an aqueous extract derived from egg shell (ES) waste. This study examines the use of TiO2 NPs for detecting Level I-III fingerprint (FP) features via the powder dusting method. The NPs enhance the visualization of ridge patterns, minutiae points, and pore structures in latent prints. Results show improved clarity, making this method promising for high-resolution forensic FP analysis. This paper explores the use of YOLOv8x, a cutting-edge object detection model, for FP analysis. YOLOv8x advanced architecture allows for rapid and accurate identification of minutiae points, ridge patterns, and other essential FP features. Its high precision and real-time detection enhance the efficiency of FP recognition, making it applicable to forensic investigations and biometric authentication. Experimental results show that YOLOv8x outperforms traditional methods in both accuracy and processing speed, highlighting its superiority in FP analysis tasks. The PVA@TiO2 films are engineered to offer excellent transparency, exceptional flexibility, and efficient UV radiation blocking properties, which have been rigorously evaluated across the UV-A, UV-B, and UV-C spectra. Blocking experiments revealed that the PVA@TiO2 (16 wt%) film effectively blocked 90.11 % of UV-A radiation, 92.82 % of UV-B radiation and 92.72 % of UV-C radiation. A solution of TiO2 NPs at environmentally relevant concentrations (20–160 mg/mL) is utilized as a nano-priming agent to improve the germination and early seedling growth of horse gram seeds. Notably, root length increased by 61 %, while shoot length grew by 58 %. The vigor index, an essential indicator of seedling health and strength, is significantly greater in the treated seeds, achieving a value of 2460, compared to 945 in the control group. This study presents a multifunctional material designed for its efficacy in FP detection, UV blocking capabilities, and beneficial effects on seed germination, providing a wide range of potential applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing generation of solid waste presents a global challenge, causing environmental issues and public health concerns due to improper disposal. Food waste is the largest contributor, projected to increase by 44 % from 2005 to 2025 [1]. This rise is driven by population growth and industrial textile waste. Fresh ES, commonly found in bakeries, restaurants, and homes, show promise for biological applications [2]. Chicken ES, a bio-ceramic composite, contains 94 % calcium carbonate, 1 % calcium phosphate, and 1 % magnesium carbonate, embedded in a 4 % organic matrix [3]. Previous research has explored various uses for ES calcium carbonate, such as bio-fillers in polymer composites and heavy metal absorbents [4]. While ES waste can be converted into valuable products, improper disposal contributes to environmental degradation. Rich in calcium carbonate, ESs have also been studied for use as abrasives, organic dyes, and metal ion adsorbents, serving as effective additives in animal feed and fertilizers [5,6].

TiO2 is a polymorphic semiconductor with a wide band gap, existing in three solid phases: rutile, brookite, and anatase [7]. TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) are valuable optical materials used in heterogeneous catalysis, solar energy conversion, solar cells, optoelectronic devices, and biological applications [8]. Factors influencing the photocatalytic properties of nano-sized TiO2 include crystallinity, stability, agglomeration tendency, surface area, shape, and production method, while TiO2 NPs enhance photon detection due to their large surface-to-volume ratios and active sites [9]. The distinct properties of rutile and anatase arise from their lattice structures, with increased phonon modes as particle size decreases [10]. Methods for preparing TiO2 NPs, nanorods, and nanosheets include sol–gel [11], hydrothermal [12], mechano-chemical [13], biological [14], oxidation, sono-chemical [15], and microwave-assisted techniques. The bio-reduction process for green production of metal oxide NPs is a one-step, low-energy, and cost-effective method [16,17].

FPs patterns are unique skin lines created by secretions after contact with surfaces, serving as distinct biometric identifiers [18]. LFP technology utilizes this uniqueness for identification and verification, aiding criminal investigations. Traditional FP detection methods, like silver nitrate and ninhydrin, face limitations such as toxicity and low sensitivity [19,20]. Recently, rare earth-doped luminescent materials have emerged as a research focus in LFP detection due to their exceptional luminosity, which reduces background interference [21]. Further, YOLOv8x is an advanced object detection algorithm optimized for high accuracy and real-time performance, particularly in forensic applications like FP detection [22]. It analyzes ridge patterns and minutiae points for accurate identification and matching. Redmon et al. [23] introduced YOLOv8x, an end-to-end object detection framework inspired by the human visual system's ability to capture visual information at a single glance. Inspired by the human visual system, YOLOv8x efficiently processes complex or low-quality images, enhancing the speed and accuracy of FP analysis. This advancement in machine learning significantly improves the effectiveness of forensic investigations and biometric security systems [24].

Recently, there has been significant focus on developing UV-shielding materials to protect against harmful UV radiation, classified into organic and inorganic types. Organic materials, such as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films with embedded molecules, can effectively block UV light below 300 nm, as demonstrated by films containing low concentrations of Sepia eumelanin [25]. Dopamine-melanin hollow nanoparticles in PVA films also provide excellent UV protection while maintaining visible light transparency. In contrast, inorganic UV-shielding materials are preferred for their superior optical and chemical stability, broader shielding spectrum, and lower toxicity. The rise of electronic devices like smartphones, CFLs, and LEDs has increased human exposure to UV radiation [26], highlighting the need for effective UV-blocking materials to prevent harm to human eyes, fruits, and polymers [27]. While traditional UV absorbers like silicon dioxide, titanium dioxide, and zinc oxide are commonly used, they often have limitations, including low flame retardancy and potential polymer degradation [28]. This has spurred demand for UV-absorbing materials like TiO2 NPs, which offer high environmental and biological compatibility while maintaining visible spectrum transparency. Table 1
[29–42] offers a detailed overview of the applications of TiO2 NPs across various fields.

Rapid seed germination and plant development are essential for agricultural production and profitability [43]. While many Asian farmers traditionally use direct seeding for important crops, this method can be ineffective in unfavorable conditions, and old seeds can further decrease germination uniformity and seedling establishment [44,45]. To enhance seed vigor, the “seed priming” approach involves soaking seeds in a solution for a specific time under controlled conditions, initiating germination without full sprouting [46]. This method stimulates pre-germinative metabolism, improving germination rates, seedling emergence, and resilience to environmental stress [47]. Identifying effective priming chemicals to enhance plant resistance in challenging conditions is vital for seed companies [48]. Common priming methods include osmo-priming, nutritional priming, hormonal priming, and hydropriming, with the patenting of specialized techniques underscoring the need for new priming agents and methods in agriculture [49].

This study thoroughly examines TiO2 NPs, emphasizing their applications and properties. We explored their potential in advanced forensic applications like FP detection using the YOLOv8x deep learning model for swift identification. Additionally, we assessed TiO2 NPs' UV-blocking capabilities for protection against harmful radiation and their effects on seed germination and plant growth. Ultimately, the TiO2 NPs demonstrate successful applications in FP detection, UV blocking, and seed germination.

The experimental procedure for the collection and preparation of ES powder involves several key steps. First, clean ES are collected and thoroughly washed with distilled water to remove any residual organic matter. The washed ES are then allowed to air dry at room temperature until completely dry. Once dried, the ES are placed in a hot air oven preheated to 120 °C and calcined 800 °C for 3 h to convert CaCO3 in the ES into CaO and remove any organic impurities. This step further dries the shells and makes them brittle for easier grinding. After cooling, the ES are ground into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle. The ground powder is then filtered through a sieve to ensure uniform particle size. Finally, the filtered ES powder is stored in a desiccator to prevent moisture absorption and maintain its stability until further use. The schematic representation is depicted in Fig. 1 a.

ES powder extract (10 mL) is added to a 0.5 M titanium oxy solution and stirred for 30 min at 60 °C. To correct and preserve the pH ∼ 8, 1 M NaOH solution is then progressively added. This process helps control the reaction conditions and ensures the optimal chemical environment for the formation of the desired titanium compound. After synthesis, wash the TiO2 product with distilled ethanol to remove any unreacted titanium precursors, calcium residues, or other impurities. Filter the solution to collect the TiO2 NPs. The ES powder is repeatedly cleaned with Milli-Q water to get rid of extra NaOH. After 2–3 h of drying, the resultant product is calcined at 850 °C for 3 h, yielding TiO2 NPs (Fig. 1b).

The LFPs development procedure involved using TiO2 via the powder dusting method on FPs provided by a 30-year-old donor. Initially, the donor pressed their finger onto a clean, smooth surface to deposit LFPs. High-purity TiO2 powder is then gently applied to the surface using a soft brush, ensuring even coverage without damaging the FPs ridges. Excess powder is carefully brushed away, revealing the FPs ridges where the TiO2 adhered to the residues. The developed FPs are subsequently captured using a Canon EOS 700D digital camera under daylight conditions, with the camera settings optimized to accurately capture the fine details of the FPs ridges. The images are then documented and stored for further analysis.

The experimental procedure for fabricating the film began with the preparation of a PVA solution by dissolving 1.5 g of PVA in 20 mL of water, followed by vigorous stirring at 80 °C for 1 h to ensure complete dissolution. In parallel, 2 mg of TiO2 is dispersed in 10 mL of deionized (DI) water and stirred at 80 °C for 1 h to achieve a uniform dispersion. After both solutions are prepared, they are combined and the mixture is stirred at 100 °C for 2 h to ensure thorough integration of the TiO2 into the PVA matrix. The resulting solution is then poured into petri plates and allowed to dry for 3 days under ambient conditions, forming a composite film of PVA and TiO2@PVA. Once fully dried, the film is carefully peeled off from the petri plates for further analysis or use (Fig. 1c).

Initially, all selected seeds are sterilized in a mercuric chloride (HgCl2) solution for 5 min to eliminate bacterial contamination. The seeds are then rinsed with deionized water for 10–15 mins to remove any residual HgCl2. To start the sprouting process, the dried seeds are immersed in distilled water for a whole day. The seeds are then moved to Petri dishes containing TiO2 solutions at different concentrations (20, 40, 60, 80, and 160 mg/mL) once they had completely sprouted. The selection of concentrations is determined based on preliminary experiments. To ensure consistency in measurements, 25 seeds are placed in each Petri dish, with each dish having 5 mL of the NPs solution (aq). Each Petri dish included 5 mL of the aqueous NPs solution, and 25 seeds are added to each dish to guarantee consistency in measurements. The control group consisted of seeds soaked in DIwater. Employing thread, the roots of the matured seeds are determined. Following 5 to 10 days of the incubation process a variety of root pairs and leaves which emerged therefrom the germinated seeds are measured for examination (Fig. 1d).

Horse gram seedlings from different TiO2 doses are randomly selected, and the length and diameter of their roots and shoots are measured using a ruler and a vernier caliper on the 5th and 10th days. After being weighed to ascertain their fresh weight, the corresponding roots and shoots are kept at a consistent 60 °C for 2 days in a drying box. After drying, the samples are weighed again to record their dry weight.

In this, Lt and Lc stand for the length of the germination of the seeds in the TiO2 and control samples, respectively, while Sc and St stand for the total number of germination in the TiO2 and control samples, respectively. For both the TiO2 and control samples, the average root length of the seeds is shown by the metrics Cc and Cd. The variable n represents the quantity of seeds that germinated on day d, while the variable d indicates the number of days since germination started.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of chlorophyll content in leaves of horse gram
ce:section.ce:para:
The chlorophyll content is determined by analysing leaves of sprouted horse gram exposed to different concentrations of TiO2 in a deionized water system. The photosynthetic pigment content is extracted using the method outlined by Harborne [51]. Initially, fully green leaves from sprouted horse gram are immersed in 95 % ethanol to initiate partial hydration. Following this, the leaves are finely ground into a paste to facilitate the extraction process. The resulting paste is then immersed in 85 % ethanol to ensure complete lysis of the leaf cells. This extended treatment facilitated the thorough extraction of photosynthetic pigments and other cellular components. The paste is left in the dark for 45 min, followed by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The chlorophyll content is measured using the formulas outlined by Arnon and Lichtenthaler [52].

The phase purity and crystal structure are assessed using a Shimadzu 7000 PXRD. The instrument is set to operate at 45 kV and 30 mA, using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) and a nickel filter. The scanning is conducted at a rate of 2°/min with a step interval of 0.02° across the 10–80°. Functional group analysis is performed using an FTIR spectrophotometer, with KBr as a reference, on a PerkinElmer instrument. The light absorption properties are assessed using an UV spectrophotometer (UV–vis: Shimadzu UV-2550, Japan). A Thermo Scientific K-Alpha electron spectrometer with a step size of 1 eV and a range of 0–1200 eV is used to examine the surface chemistry of the samples. A Hitachi 3000 scanning electron microscope is used to study the NPs surface morphology. Additionally, the EDAX Apollo XT EDS equipment is used to evaluate the elemental composition of the NPs. Using a JEOL JEM-2100 Plus TEM operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images are acquired. Raman spectra of the samples, covering the range of 100–800 cm−1, are obtained using a Horiba Jobin-Yvon LabRam HR Raman spectrometer.

Fig. 2 a illustrates the PXRD patterns of pure TiO2 NPs. The diffraction peaks indicate the presence of the anatase phase, which corresponds well with JCPDS file no. 89–4202 [53]. The synthesized material exhibits only the anatase TiO2 phase, with no presence of the rutile phase. Crystallite size can be estimated using Scherrer's equation, which is a widely used method in XRD analysis. Scherrer's equation relates the crystallite size to the broadening of a diffraction peak. The crystallite size D is calculated using the following formula [54]:
(11)
D =
k λ
β C o s θ
where, ‘D’ is the crystallite size (nm). ‘K’ is the shape factor, often taken as 0.9 (though it can vary depending on the crystal shape). ‘λ’ is the X-ray wavelength (nm). Commonly used X-ray sources include Cu Kα with λ = 0.15406 nm. ‘β' is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak (rad). ‘θ’ is the Bragg angle corresponding to the peak (in radians). Crystallite size can be determined from the width of the reflected peak. The width of the diffraction line can be evaluated using either the FWHM or the integrated breadth methods [36]. In this case, the crystallite size is estimated using the FWHM. Various XRD analysis methods for calculating crystallite size, including the Williamson-Hall (W-H), Halder-Wagner (H-W), and Size-Strain plot (SS), are discussed in the following sections. All the data are summarized in Table 2 .

Grain boundaries, stacking faults, and micro-strain resulting from particlesdefects are examples of lattice microstructures that are not taken into consideration by the Scherrer'sequation. It merely discusses how the size of the crystallite affects the broadening of XRD peaks [55]. The Williamson-Hall (WH) method takes strain-induced broadening into account by computing both crystallite size and intrinsic strain. Because it considers both strain and size as parameters that contribute to XRD peak broadening, this technique is thought to be more accurate and desirable. To differentiate between the effects of strain and size, the WH technique uses the tangent function, in contrast to Scherrer's method, which uses a 1/Cosθ connection. Smaller crystallite size and micro-strain are combined with WH to divide the broadening into discrete reflections. The W-H method Eq (12) distinguishes between effects connected to strain and size [56]:
(12)
β
total

β

(13)
β r
C o s θ =

+ η s i n θ
where β is the strain-induced broadening of the peak width. Eq (12) can be applied to orientated anatase peaks and takes crystal anisotropy into consideration. The x-axis in Fig. 2b displays 4sinθ, while the y-axis displays βCosθ. With this method, strain is represented by the slope and crystallite size is represented by the intercept of the fitted line, with all crystalline peaks aligned with the (hkl) plane. Because of the reduced quality of XRD data, peak overlap at higher angles, and increased diffraction, less weight is given to data from high-angle reflections. Since there is stronger diffraction, peak overlaps at higher angles, and lower XRD accuracy of the data, this approach gives less weight to data from high-angle reflections. With regard to isotropic broadening, this method yields more precise results. The formula you provided represents the total peak broadening βtotal) as a sum of two components: Lorentzian broadening (βL) and Gaussian broadening (βG). This approach is commonly used when the broadening can be modelled by both Lorentzian and Gaussian distributions, such as in the Voigt profile, which is a convolution of these two types of broadening. However, to combine Lorentzian and Gaussian components correctly, you typically use a quadratic sum rather than a simple addition, as follows [57]:
(14)
β
total
=
β L
+
β G
here, βtotal is the total observed peak broadening. βL is the Lorentzian component of the broadening, often associated with factors like crystallite size. βG is the Gaussian component of the broadening, which can be due to factors like micro-strain or instrumental effects. The SSP method is an alternative approach to Scherrer’s equation for estimating the crystallite size and micro-strain from XRD data. The SSP method considers both the broadening due to crystallite size and micro-strain and presents them in a linear form for easier analysis. Equation (15) is used to calculate the crystallite size in the SSP method [58].
(15)

The SSP approach indicates that a Gaussian or Lorentzian function must account for strain and crystal size broadening in the XRD peak profile. However, the XRD peak profile is unable to display both of these features at the same time. For XRD peak broadening, the symmetric Voigt function is recommended by the H-W model as an alternative to the Gaussian and Lorentzian functions in order to get across this limitation. Due to this approach, the H-W method can now accurately depict the full width at half maximum of the XRD profile according to the Voigt function [59]:

High-resolution XPS analysis of TiO2 NPs provides detailed information on the electronic states and chemical environments of titanium and oxygen in the material (Fig. S1a). The Ti 2p region of the XPS spectrum shows characteristic peaks for titanium, with a prominent doublet corresponding to Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 (Fig. S1b). The observed separation between these peaks is 5.44 eV, which aligns with the reported value for TiO2, confirming the presence of titanium in its typical oxidation state (Ti4+). This separation confirms the presence of TiO2 in its anatase or rutile phase [62]. The O 1 s spectrum reveals three distinct peaks (Fig. S1c). The peak at 530.0 eV is attributed to Ti4+-O, indicating lattice oxygen in the TiO2 crystal structure. This is a key feature of the TiO2 lattice, representing the oxygen atoms bonded to titanium within the crystal. The peak 531.0 eV corresponds to Ti3+-O, which is associated with oxygen vacancies or deficient oxygen sites within the TiO2 lattice. The presence of this peak suggests that there are some reduced titanium sites or lattice defects. Also, the peak at 532.3 eV is assigned to O-H groups [63], which represent surface oxygen species such as hydroxyl groups and adsorbed water molecules. This peak indicates the presence of surface hydroxyl groups and water on the nanoparticle surfaces, which can affect the material's reactivity and stability. In Fig. S1d, the C 1 s peak is resolved into two Gaussian components. The primary peak at a binding energy of 284.73 eV is attributed to CC/CH bonds, while the smaller peak at 286.92 eV corresponds to COC bonds [64]. Overall, the XPS analysis provides insights into the oxidation states of titanium and the different types of oxygen environments in TiO2 NPs, contributing to the understanding of their structural and chemical properties.

Fig. S2a-b displays the surface morphology of anatase TiO2 as observed by FE-SEM. The micrographs reveal that the particles are agglomerated and have a spherical shape. FE-SEM is employed to examine the surface morphology and particle size distribution of TiO2 NPs. The EDS method is utilized to perform quantitative elemental microanalysis of the prepared NPs. Elemental mapping during the EDS analysis is conducted, and the results are presented in Fig. S2
c. Typical EDS patterns reveal two prominent peaks at approximately 0.5 eV and 4.5 eV, confirming the presence of titanium and oxygen, respectively. The spectra reveal that TiO2 has a stoichiometric ratio of approximately Ti (∼35.15 %) and O (∼64.85 %). EDS analysis of the surface areas indicates that the atomic ratio of Ti to O is close to 1:2, with both elements being uniformly distributed across each sample. The elemental mapping image of TiO2 ( Fig. S2
d, e) reveals a consistent distribution of titanium throughout the sample, demonstrating successful synthesis and a uniform distribution of elements [65]. This observation significantly strengthens the integrity, accuracy, reliability, and purity of the synthesized TiO2 NPs.

The microscopic structures of the catalysts are further analyzed using TEM and HR-TEM techniques. TEM analysis provided insights into the particle size, growth patterns, and distribution of crystallites (Fig. 3 a). The SAED patterns indicate that the samples are well-crystallized, aligning with the PXRD analysis results. Fig. 3b presents the SAED pattern of TiO2 NPs, revealing diffraction rings characteristic of the anatase phase. These rings suggest that the particles exhibit good crystallization and a narrow size distribution. Each SAED ring corresponds to specific diffraction peaks of anatase TiO2. The lattice fringes measured indicate that the anatase TiO2 NPs have d-spacing values of 0.352, 0.246, 0.237, 0.257, 0.189, 0.170, 0.1664, 0.148, 0.136, 0.133, and 0.126 nm, which align with the (101), (103), (004), (112), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220), and (215) crystallographic planes, respectively. The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 3c reveals distinctly separated and crystalline TiO2 NPs, as indicated by the d-spacing measurements. The HRTEM image clearly displays lattice fringes with spacings of 0.352 nm and 0.237 nm, corresponding to the (101) and (004) plane orientations of anatase TiO2, respectively. For crystallographic analysis of the images, the GM3 digital micrograph software (Gatan Microscopy, USA) [66] is utilized to perform a Fast Fourier Transformation (FFT) on a selected region of the image. This technique provided a representation of reciprocal space, which is used to identify the crystalline lattices and create a corresponding mask. Subsequently, the Inverse Fast Fourier Transform (IFFT) tool is employed to extract images from three distinct crystalline regions. By applying a line profile to the inverse FFT images and utilizing the GATAN software, we are able to calculate the d-spacing values. The results revealed that the d-spacing of the atomic planes is 0.352 nm (as shown in the inset of Fig. 3d) and 0.237 nm (as depicted in the inset of Fig. 3e). These d-spacing values correspond to the (101) and (004) planes, respectively.

Fig. S3a illustrates the Raman spectra of pure TiO2 NPs, acquired within the wavenumber range of 0 to 1000 cm−1. The Raman spectrum reveals key vibrational modes associated with the TiO2 structure. The prominent peaks observed are characteristic of the anatase phase of TiO2, which is commonly studied in nanoparticle form. The main Raman-active modes include defined by the irreducible representation 1A1g + 1A2u + 2B1g + 1B2u + 3Eg + 2Eu. In this representation, A1g, B1g, and Eg are Raman-active modes, A2u and Eu are infrared-active modes, while the B2u mode is inactive for both Raman and infrared. The Raman-active modes are observed at 142, 198, 395, 515, and 636 cm−1. The most prominent and sharp peak observed at 142 cm−1 is assigned to the anatase phase of TiO2. According to Ohsaka [67], the Raman spectrum of anatase single crystals reveals six active modes: 142, 198, and 636 cm−1 for the Eg mode; 395 cm−1 for the B1g mode; and 515 cm−1 for the A1g and B1g modes. At a low temperature of 75 K, this peak separates into 515 cm−1 (A1g), 519 cm−1 (B1g), and 636 cm−1 (Eg) peaks. However, at room temperature, the Raman spectra do not reveal any peaks associated with impurities in pure TiO2 NPs. These observations for the anatase samples are consistent with the XRD results [68].

Fig. S3b presents the FT-IR absorption spectrum of the prepared TiO2 samples, covering the wavenumber range from 400 cm−1 to 800 cm−1. The FT-IR spectrum reveals distinct absorption bands within this range, which are primarily attributed to the vibrational modes of TiO and TiOO bonds [69]. The stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups (–OH) typically appear around 3502 cm−1. This broad peak suggests the presence of surface hydroxyl groups or water molecules associated with the TiO2 surface. The TiO2 sample features adsorbed carboxylate species, likely from organic acids, indicated by a peak around 1558 cm−1. This peak suggests the presence of asymmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxylate (COO–) group. The peak at 1197 cm−1 in the spectrum likely signifies the presence of organic or surface-bound species, such as C–O stretching vibrations from organic contaminants or adsorbed carbonate species [70].

UV–Vis spectroscopy is well-regarded for its sensitivity and effectiveness in characterizing the optical properties of NPs. As depicted in Fig. S3c, the TiO2 NPs samples are analyzed under UV and visible light across the wavelength range of 300–800 nm at room temperature. When an electron is excited from the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) to the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO), the energy needed is typically referred to as the band-gap energy [71]. The UV–Vis spectrum evidences light energy absorption, according to the data. Tauc's relation [72] is used to estimate the optical band gap of a semiconductor material from its absorption spectrum. It involves analyzing the plot of the absorption coefficient versus photon energy to determine the band gap energy.
(24)
α h ν = A
h ν -
E g
n
where, α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy, Eg ​ is the optical band gap of the material, A is a constant and n depends on the type of electronic transition (direct or indirect). The band gap is determined by calculating (αhν)1 / n for various values of n and photon energies. The band gap value is then found by extrapolating a linear segment of (αhν)2 versus hν, in accordance with the Tauc’s relation. Fig. S3d shows the absorption spectra and Tauc’s plots for these samples. The band-gap energies of optical transitions are determined by the point where the dotted straight line intersects the horizontal axis. Consequently, the energy band gap of TiO2 is found to be 3.46 eV.

A powder dusting method is employed to assess the durability of optimized TiO2 NPs on different surfaces, including leaf, aluminum foil, and glass slides, under UV radiation at 365 nm (Fig. 4 a–c). The images reveal that the NPs provide noise-free backgrounds, high sensitivity, contrast, and clear visualization of FP details. Using ImageJ [73], plotting the pixel profiles of a specific region (white strip) from each FP enables comparison (Fig. 4a1–c1). The image has higher resolution due to the greater contrast between the FPs ridges and furrows, resulting in a wider variation in gray values (GV) between them. The chosen area's pixel profile (the white strip) is shown in Fig. 4a1–c1. A pixel's brightness in an image can vary from 0 to 150, representing the brightest to the darkest. The application of uniformly distributed illuminating powder on ridges instead of grooves is seen in the accompanying 3D interactive profile (Fig. 4a11–c11).

FPs extraction from glass surfaces using TiO2 NPs presents a significant advancement in forensic science, demonstrating substantial potential for application in criminal investigations. TiO2 NPs enhance the visualization of FPs features on challenging glass surface, where traditional methods may fall short. By applying a TiO2 nanoparticle suspension to the glass, LFPs become more discernible under visible light. This technique effectively highlights Level I features, such as ridge patterns. Both level I features (core) and level II details (crossovers, islands, bifurcations, eyes, enclosures, hooks, incipient ridges, and ridge ends) are distinctly visualized. Remarkably, level 3 details, including scars and sweat pores, are also clearly revealed with the use of white fluorescent TiO2 powder (Fig. 5 a). The method's ability to improve detect minutia details makes it invaluable for forensic analysis, offering a non-destructive, sensitive, and versatile tool that enhances the accuracy and effectiveness of FP-based evidence in criminal investigations. Thus, the LFP developed with the prepared TiO2 powder showed exceptional resolution, highlighting its substantial potential for application in criminal investigations [74–77]. Pixel profiles of ridges and furrows in FPs images are analyzed using ImageJ software to investigate the intensity variations and structural characteristics of the FP patterns. The analysis aimed to provide a detailed understanding of the ridge and furrow structures by plotting the pixel intensity profiles along specific lines (Fig. 5b). The resulting pixel profiles revealed distinct patterns corresponding to the ridges and furrows. Typically, ridges, being elevated and more pronounced, showed higher intensity values, whereas furrows, being recessed, exhibited lower intensity values. This contrast allowed for clear visualization of the FP structural details, such as ridge width, depth, and spacing. From the results the use of ImageJ for plotting pixel profiles provided a powerful tool for examining and understanding the fine details of FP ridges and furrows, enhancing the effectiveness of FP analysis and forensic investigations.

It is frequently challenging to identify the crime location and collect evidence. For criminal investigations to be effective, aged FPs must be produced. The LFPs are gathered on aluminum foil and stored for 1, 60, 120, and 180 days at room temperature [78–80]. The stored LFPs are subsequently developed using TiO2 powder via the powder dusting method. As depicted in Fig. S4(a–d), images of the 1, 60, 120, and 180-day aged samples, when viewed under a 365 nm UV lamp, displayed nearly identical patterns, revealing all the ridge features. The 3-D interactive plots of the FPs demonstrate a consistent distribution of TiO2 powder across the ridge surfaces (Fig. S4a1–d1). This suggests that TiO2 powder remains effective for developing FPs, even for samples aged up to 180 days, which is a significant finding [81].

Standard reagents, including Rhodamine 6G (red), Sudan Green (green), and Crystal Violet (blue) commercial powders, are used as controls to compare the performance of TiO2 NPs for visualizing LFPs, as illustrated in Fig. S4(e–g). The right side of Fig. S4(e, f & g) demonstrates that standard reagents can reveal level-1 features. However, the local ridges tend to adhere to each other, which obscures the detailed ridge regions, such as level-2 and level-3 features, making them less clearly visible. In contrast, the TiO2 NPs, shown on the left side of Fig. S4(e, f & g), clearly distinguish between ridge and furrow regions, with detailed identification of level-1 through level-3 features [82]. Therefore, TiO2 NPs demonstrate superior properties compared to standard reagents for LFP visualization, indicating that TiO2 NPs are a promising material for this application. The gray value analysis is presented in Fig. S4(e1- g1).

Examining crime scene prints that are hazy, incomplete, overlapping, unclearly oriented, or devoid of discernible details is where poroscopy shines [83]. Although the crime scene prints from the Boudet and Simonin instances did not exhibit a clear overall pattern, 901 and 2000 sweat pores are found in similar relative positions for the two cases, respectively [84,85]. A partial FP showing 200 sweat pores that is found at the crime scene in the Matern theft case matched Matern's right ring finger. Numerous factors, such as the number of sweat pores (per unit area and length), their form, size, location (central or peripheral), type (closed, open at one end, or open at both ends), and spacing, can be used to assess sweat pores. The distinctive featuresof sweat pores will be covered in detail in the sections that follow. The LFPs generated on an aluminum foil surface are displayed in Fig. 6 a. There are two ways to assess the pore count: calculating the average number of pores per unit area and length. Twelve pores are found for every centimeter of the epidermal ridge (Fig. 6b). Bindra et al. [86] found 8–25 sweat pores per centimeter of friction ridge, which is consistent with their findings. The number of sweat pores per unit length has been discovered to be stable throughout time, which is significant. The size of sweat pores has been the subject of several investigations; in the work we conducted, the pore diameters varied between 300 and 500 μm. Pore size can vary and is not always repeatable, according to earlier research. Additionally, research involving hydrochromic sensors to track sweat pore activity suggests that pore size may vary with physiological changes. Consequently, pore size alone should not be used as the sole criterion for matching FPs. Sweat pores can be located in various positions on the papillary ridge, including near the edges or centrally.

Importantly, central sweat pores are often closed, which can influence their visibility and interpretation. This variability means that pore size, while informative, is not always a reliable or definitive factor in FP matching. To ensure accurate identification and comparison, it is essential to consider additional features and contextual information about the sweat pores and overall ridge patterns. Conversely, pores near the periphery may be open on one side or both sides (Fig. 6b). Closed pores are generally more common than those that are open on one or both sides. It is important to understand that the appearance of a pore as open or closed is not a result of its intrinsic structure but rather due to the way ink adheres to its edges. In simpler terms, while a sweat pore is naturally closed, it might look open on one or both sides due to NPs adhering to its edges. Factors such as the pressure and angle during print deposition, the consistency of the TiO2 powder, and the method used for developing the prints can influence the appearance of the pore, potentially affecting whether it is classified as closed, open on one side, or open on both sides [87]. When a pore becomes distorted, its shape, size, and type can change due to the elasticity and compression of the skin, causing it to stretch (Fig. 6b). Nevertheless, the spacing between pores remains fairly consistent and falls within a reproducible range [88]. Additionally, despite noticeable changes in ridge patterns due to pressure distortions, the relative positions of the pores remain consistent during flexion. As a result, the distance between the center of one pore and the center of an adjacent pore remains stable for each individual. As a result, connecting the centers of sweat pores along the same ridge forms a distinct and individual-specific pattern. Similarly, linking the center of one pore to the center of another on a parallel ridge generates a unique configuration. Research has confirmed the reliability of analyzing pore interspacing, and this study further supports the use of pore inter-distance as a method for FP individualization. The probability of finding 12 consecutive pores arranged in an identical configuration on a single ridge is exceptionally low, highlighting the uniqueness and distinctiveness of sweat pore patterns. Additionally, the variability in pore area is significantly affected by the methods used for deposition and development, as illustrated in Fig. 6c. Detailed measurements of pore area are documented in Table 3 , providing comprehensive data on the variations observed.

Sweat pores exhibit a variety of shapes, including square, rhomboid, triangular, oval, elliptical, and round. Bindra et al. [86] identify oval, triangular, and rectangular pores as the next most common shapes after the more prevalent round ones. Recent studies [89] indicate that triangular and rectangular pores are relatively rare, whereas round and oval pores are more frequently observed. It's important to note that while sweat pores are inherently round or oval, the appearance of these pores can be influenced by TiO2 NPs. Depending on how these NPs adhere to the edges of the pores and deposit on the surface, they can alter the pore’s shape, making it appear triangular, rectangular, oval, or elliptical, as illustrated in Fig. 6d. In essence, while sweat pores are primarily oval or circular, the adherence of TiO2 NPs to their edges can make them appear triangular, rectangular, rhomboid, or assume other shapes. Additionally, the shape of pores is not always consistent, as pressure-induced distortions can alter their fundamental form [90]. To evaluate the quality of the produced FPs, grayscale value analysis is conducted using ImageJ software. This analysis emphasizes the differences between ridges and furrows, as demonstrated in Fig. 6e.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of YOLOv8x for detection and analysis of latent fingerprints
ce:section.ce:para: The YOLOv8x model, a state-of-the-art object detection algorithm, has several promising applications for the detection and analysis of LFPs. To utilize YOLOv8x for FP analysis, the algorithm is initially trained on a dataset of FP images, with each image annotated with bounding boxes highlighting minute details. During this training phase, the network is exposed to a large volume of FP images and their corresponding annotations, allowing it to learn and recognize the intricate patterns and unique features of FP minutiae [91]. Once the YOLOv8x model has been successfully trained, it can accurately identify and locate minute details in new, previously unseen FP images. The algorithm processes the input FP image through pooling and convolutional layers to extract relevant features. Based on these features, it predicts the presence and exact locations of minutiae points, which are indicated by bounding boxes. This capability allows automated FP recognition systems to perform effectively across various applications, including access control, law enforcement, and biometric identification [92]. Fig. 7 in the paper illustrates the analytical process involved in using YOLOv8x for object detection, which comprises several distinct steps.

Preprocessing: After preprocessing the input image, it is fed into the YOLOv8x model for analysis. During this process, the model performs a forward pass, where the image is passed through multiple layers of the neural network. These layers extract features at various scales, capturing both low-level details and more complex patterns. The model then produces predictions, which include bounding boxes to localize potential objects, class probabilities to identify each object, and confidence scores estimating the likelihood of the predictions being correct (Fig. 7 a).

Training model: Depending on the requirements of the task, the YOLOv8x model can be trained from beginning or initialized with pretrained weights, as shown in Fig. 7b. By using pretrained measurements, the model can benefit from prior training experience on a big dataset, giving it a strong starting point. However, when working with specialized data that is very different from the pretraining data, training the model from beginning entails learning straight from the dataset, which might be helpful. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are highly effective for FP detection due to their ability to automatically learn and extract features from images. CNNs are a class of deep learning models specifically designed to process grid-like data, such as images. They use layers of convolutions, pooling, and activation functions to automatically learn and extract hierarchical features from the input images [93]. Through numerous layers of convolution, pooling, and activation functions, this architecture processes visual data to enable the YOLOv8 model to locate and identify objects within images with efficiency.

Prediction: The YOLOv8x model's predictions, which include bounding boxes, class probabilities, and confidence scores, go through a crucial post-processing stage, as shown in Fig. 7c. Through the removal of low-confidence detections and the refinement of the bounding box coordinates, this phase seeks to increase the accuracy of the model. Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS), which is essential for resolving situations where numerous bounding boxes overlap and represent the same object, is a significant approach utilized during this stage [94]. Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) reduces duplication and improves output precision by keeping just the most confident detections by analyzing the confidence scores of these overlapping boxes. The output of the YOLOv8x analysis is a well-organized list of items that have been spotted, each with a matching bounding box and class label. Because of their extreme adaptability, these results can be easily integrated into a wide range of downstream procedures. They can be employed in more complex applications like object tracking, recognition, or classification, or visualized for simple interpretation in other computational tasks. YOLOv8x precise item detection and localization make it a priceless tool in a variety of fields. Visual representation of Bifurcation, ridge end, loop and scar FP features obtained from YOLOv8x program are shown in Fig. S7.

Metrics like F1-score, mean average precision (mAP), recall (R), and precision (P) are used to assess how well the trained system performs. True positives (TP), true negatives (TN), false positives (FP), and false negatives (FN) are the basis on which these metrics are further defined [95].
True positives (TP): The bounding box of the forged object is accurately identified and predicted by the network, matching the ground truth.
True negatives (TN): When forged objects are absent from the ground truth, the model does not produce any bounding boxes for them.
False positives (FP): When the trained model falsely assigns a labeled bounding box to an item that is not forged, this prediction may result in type I errors.
False negatives (FN): Type II mistakes occur when the trained model is unable to anticipate a bounding box around a forged object in a frame where the ground truth suggests forgery.

Precision (P): It calculates the frequency with which the trained model predicts correctly. Increased precision indicates the trustworthiness of the model and is beneficial for system correctness [96].
(25)
P r e c i s i o n
=
TP
Tp + F P

Recall (R): The percentage of accurate positive forecasts among all real positives is measured by a metric called recall. For the purpose of increasing the accuracy of the system, a higher recall is preferable as it denotes more effectiveness.
(26)
R e c a l l
=
TP
Tp + F N
F1 Score: It determines the threshold of confidence scores that, when accuracy and recall are optimized, results in the maximum F1 score. A high F1 score denotes high values for both precision and recall, and vice versa, since the F1 score balances these two factors [97].
(27)
F 1 =
2 ∗ P ∗ R
P + R
Intersection of union (IOU): By calculating the overlap between the predicted and ground truth boxes, IOU evaluates the precision of a predicted bounding box. More overlap is indicated by a higher IOU, as Fig. 7d illustrates. A higher IOU is ideal for evaluating the model's efficacy. An IOU above 0.5 validates a true positive, while an IOU below 0.5 indicates a false positive. The IOU threshold is set at 0.5.

Mean average precision (mAP): Mean Average Precision (mAP) is calculated by first determining the Average Precision (AP) for each class and then averaging these values across multiple classes (N = 7). It is a standard metric used to evaluate the accuracy of an object detection model [98].
(28)
mAP =
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
A
P i
Inference Speed: The average number of iterations processed per second is used to measure inference speed, which affects how rapidly input is processed and output is produced.

Confidence Score: The bounding box's estimated probability around the forged object is accurately represented by the confidence score. It is computed by dividing the bounding box's width and height by the image's width and height. The range of the confidence score is 0 to 1.

This model is likely designed to detect and classify FP patterns using the YOLOv8 architecture. It exhibits strong performance in terms of precision and recall, as evidenced by the confusion matrices and graphs. Bifurcation refers to a point where a single ridge divides into two separate ridges (Fig. S5a). This is a critical minutiae feature used in FP recognition. Ridge End is another essential feature where a ridge terminates. These points are also key in identifying unique FP patterns (Fig. S5g). The confusion matrices illustrate the accuracy of the model in detecting Bifurcation and Ridge End features versus background noise. Bifurcation Confusion Matrix achieves an 89 % accuracy in correctly identifying Bifurcation features (true positive rate). It also accurately identifies background regions 100 % of the time (true negative rate). However, 11 % of the time, the model mistakenly classifies background areas as Bifurcation (false positive rate), with no instances of missing Bifurcation features (false negative rate = 0 %) (Fig. S5b). Ridge End Confusion Matrix shows an 84 % accuracy in identifying Ridge End features (true positive rate). Like in the Bifurcation matrix, the model accurately identifies the background 100 % of the time (true negative rate). The model mistakenly identifies background regions as Ridge End features 16 % of the time (false positive rate), again with no false negatives (Fig. S5h).

Precision, indicating the accuracy of the positive predictions, increases consistently throughout the training process for both Bifurcation and Ridge End features. The upward trends indicate that the model reduces the number of false positives as training progresses (Fig. S5c & i). Recall shows the model’s ability to correctly identify all positive instances (true positives). For both features, recall improves over the training epochs, indicating that the model becomes better at capturing all relevant minutiae in the FP images (Fig. S5(d & j)). mAP50 assesses the model's performance at an IoU (Intersection over Union) threshold of 0.50, ensuring that predictions overlap sufficiently with the ground truth [99]. The rising mAP50 values suggest that the model's accuracy in detecting and localizing features improves with more training (Fig. S5(e & k)). mAP50-95 evaluates performance across a range of IoU thresholds, ensuring not only detection but also precise localization. The continuous increase in this metric suggests that the model is becoming adept at both identifying and accurately placing bounding boxes around the minutiae (Fig. S5(f & l)).

Loop and Scar are specific types of FP patterns, often categorized in biometric systems. A Loop is a common FP pattern where the ridges enter from one side, curve around, and exit on the same side (Fig. S6a). A Scar indicates damage or alteration to the FP pattern due to an injury, burn, or other factors. The model seems to be trained specifically to detect these two types of patterns (Fig. S6g). This specialization suggests that the model is likely used in a specific forensic or biometric application where these patterns are of interest. A confusion matrix is a valuable tool for evaluating the performance of a classification model, such as YOLOv8x, in FP detection. It provides a detailed breakdown of the model's predictions compared to the actual labels, helping to identify areas of strength and weakness [100]. In this case, the matrices compare how well the model distinguishes between the true class (Loop or Scar) and the background (non-pattern regions). The model correctly identifies the Loop pattern 86 % of the time (true positive rate), and it correctly identifies the background 100 % of the time (true negative rate). However, 14 % of the time, it incorrectly identifies a background region as a Loop (false positive rate). The model does not mistakenly label any Loop patterns as background (false negative rate = 0 %) (Fig. S6b). For Scar patterns, the model achieves 99 % precision, meaning it is highly accurate in identifying Scar patterns. Like the Loop model, it maintains 100 % accuracy in recognizing background regions. However, it mistakenly labels 10 % of background regions as Scar patterns (false positive rate), with no false negatives. The matrices indicate the model's ability to differentiate between FP patterns and background noise. The higher the percentages in the true positive and true negative cells, the better the model is at identifying the correct class. The false positive percentages show the areas where the model may still need improvement ( Fig. S6
h).

Precision, indicating the accuracy of the positive predictions, increases consistently throughout the training process for both loop and scar features. The upward trends indicate that the model reduces the number of false positives as training progresses (Fig. S6c & i). Recall shows the model’s ability to correctly identify all positive instances (true positives). For both features, recall improves over the training epochs, indicating that the model becomes better at capturing all relevant minutiae in the FP images (Fig. S6(d & j)). mAP50 assesses the model's performance at an IoU (Intersection over Union) threshold of 0.55, ensuring that predictions overlap sufficiently with the ground truth. The rising mAP50 values suggest that the model's accuracy in detecting and localizing features improves with more training (Fig. S6 (e & k)). mAP50-95 evaluates performance across a range of IoU thresholds, ensuring not only detection but also precise localization. The continuous increase in this metric suggests that the model is becoming adept at both identifying and accurately placing bounding boxes around the minutiae (Fig. S6 (f & l)).

The results for bifurcation and ridge end features after 90 epochs are shown in Fig. S8 (a-f & g-l). The results are displayed in these figures across multiple epochs, making it possible to compare how well the improved method performed against the baseline (the original YOLOv8x). Techniques for identifying FPs work best when precision and recall curves become close to one. To be more precise, the curves (Figs. S5 and S6) with our YOLOv8x model show that during the interim, precision, recall, and mAP can all approach values of about 0.9. It's evident that the enhanced technique produces superior detection results. Following 90 training epochs, the box loss (box_loss), classification loss (cls_loss), and distribution focal loss (dfl_loss) for the modified FP-YOLO base model are shown in Fig. S8. Fig. S8 (a–b & g–h) [101] display the YOLOv8x models detection results on the training and validation sets. Additionally, the improved model offers higher confidence in the outcomes, less missed and incorrect detections and more detailed feature detection. The recall-confidence, precision-confidence, precision-recall and F1-confidence curves, as well as the models performance across various confidence levels, are shown in Fig. S8 (c-f & i-l). The optimal model performance is indicated by a confidence level of 1. Using the YOLOv8 model, Fig. S9 (a-f & g-l) displays the loss function curves for the loop and scar features, together with the precision-confidence, recall-confidence, precision-recall, and F1-confidence curves.

Fig. S10 (a & b) and S11 (a & b) present the labelled features, visually representing the identified characteristics along with their corresponding labels. The identified bifurcations, ridge ends, loopsand scars are displayed in these pictures, each with a bounding box. In the FP image, these spots indicate the locations each feature. A distinct identification number and a confidence score between 0 and 1 are written on each bounding box. The graphics show how well the YOLOv8x model detects crossover points since the bounding boxes are placed exactly over the right features in the FP image. They also demonstrate the robustness of the model, showing that it can reliably recognize a range of features even in the presence of fluctuations in image quality or structure. This confirms the practical value of the identified points for generating secure keys and demonstrates that the model has successfully generalized from the training data to effectively handle various types of FP images. The correlogram of the labels, depicted in Fig. S10 (c & d) and S11 (c & d), offers a detailed visual representation of the relationships and correlations between the detected features in the FP images. The intensity and direction of correlations between variables are reflected in the correlation coefficients, which range from −1 to 1. Shades of blue are typically used to represent positive correlations, which show that when one variable rises, the other variable also tends to rise. The degree of the correlation is indicated by the correlograms color intensity, where stronger associations are represented by darker hues. Fig. S10 (c & d) and S11 (c & d) present the correlogram, which provides a detailed visual analysis of the relationships among the identified bifurcations, ridge ends, loops, and scars in the FP image. This visual representation highlights the magnitude and direction of these correlations, demonstrating the model's effectiveness and the precision of the annotations. Furthermore, it offers valuable insights that can improve the security key generation process [102].

Flexibility is a key factor in evaluating the comfort and wearability of materials, particularly in wearable technology. It can be assessed through various attributes, including bending and stretching capabilities [103]. In reality, effective wearable devices need to possess a textile-like texture and comfort, which necessitates both flexibility and softness. This study assessed the wearable flexibility and comfort of TiO2 by examining its bending, stretching, and foldability characteristics (Fig. S12 a & c). As the concentration of the substance (0–16 wt%) increases, the transparency of the TiO2 film decreases (Fig. S12b). Higher concentrations lead to more scattering or absorption of light, reducing the ability to see through the film. This tunable transparency could be useful in applications where controlled light transmission is desired, such as in coatings, sensors, or display technologies. The film undergoes multiple folds, which simulates extreme mechanical deformation (Fig. S12c). This test demonstrates that the film can handle repeated stress without losing its structure, indicating excellent durability. It shows that the film can be used in scenarios where it will be subjected to continuous or complex folding patterns. These mechanical tests show that the TiO2 films possess a high degree of flexibility and durability, making them suitable for various applications in flexible electronics, coatings, or packaging materials where mechanical stress is a factor [104].

The ability to fold, bend, and twist without damage demonstrates that the films can maintain their structural integrity under a range of mechanical deformations. As illustrated in Fig. S13a, TiO2 exhibits exceptional pressure resistance, rebounding to its original state even after being subjected to 11 tons of pressure without collapsing. As the applied pressure increases (from 9 to 11 tons), the TiO2 film undergoes visible deformation, showing that it can withstand high compressive forces but with structural changes. Correspondingly, the TiO2 present high tensile strain (over 100 %) and strength. The TiO2 film exhibits measurable elongation under increasing loads, indicating flexibility and the ability to stretch without breaking. (Fig. S13b & c), in which a TiO2 strip (30 × 2 cm) can lift a weight of 750 g. The films ability to handle different weights (350–700 g) demonstrates its tensile strength, with slight elongation as the load increases. The TiO2 films exhibit a combination of strength, flexibility, and durability. They are able to withstand high compressive forces and tensile loads while maintaining their structural integrity [105]. This makes them suitable for use in applications that require materials to endure both compressive and tensile stresses, such as flexible electronics, protective coatings, and other advanced material technologies. The balance between rigidity and flexibility ensures that the films can adapt to various mechanical demands while maintaining performance [106].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV blocking of TiO2 composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 a depicts the schematic mechanism of PVA-derived TiO2 used in UV-blocking film applications. A UV shielding efficiency of 13.88 % is inadequate for effective UV-blocking applications. To improve this, thicker polymer films are developed using polymer solutions with TiO2 NPs at concentrations ranging from 2 % to 16 % wt. %. Fig. 8a illustrates a basic experimental setup for evaluating the UV shielding effectiveness of TiO2. In this configuration, the UV light passing through the polymer film is measured using a UV digital lux meter positioned 3.5 cm below the film holder [107]. Two types of light sources are tested: a UV lamp and ambient light. The intensity of UV light transmitted through the polymer film is measured using a digital UV lux meter. Fig. 8(b–d) show the assessment of films with varying concentrations of TiO2 under both lighting conditions. The polymer film with 2 wt% TiO2 exhibited a UV-blocking efficiency of around 58.30 % when exposed to a UV A lamp. Similarly, polymer films containing 4 wt%, 6 wt%, 8 wt%, and 16 wt% TiO2 demonstrated UV light absorption efficiencies of approximately 67.88 %, 73.19 %, 83.30 %, and 90.11 %, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 8b. Similar results are obtained from an experiment conducted in daytime (UV-B), as shown in Fig. 8c. These results show that the best UV shielding efficiency is provided by a TiO2 concentration of 16 wt%. A prominent absorption peak in the UV spectral region significantly confirms the 16 wt% TiO2 ability to block UV radiation. Similar findings are obtained from the experiment conducted in 254 nm UV-C light, as shown in Fig. 8d, with blocking percentages specified in Table 4 . These findings demonstrate that, independent of the light source, the TiO2 films UV shielding efficacy increases significantly with thickness. This demonstrates how important film weight percentage (wt%) is for increasing the films UV protection capabilities. Using a UV digital lux meter, we quantitatively assessed the polymer films UV shielding efficacy. TiO2 provides significant light scattering and reflection, which contributes to the film's enhanced UV shielding effectiveness [108].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Seed germination activity in presence of TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Before applying the synthesized TiO2 in agriculture, it is crucial to evaluate its effectiveness. For this assessment, horse gram seeds are used to investigate the effects of various TiO2 concentrations, ranging from 0 to 160 mg/mL, on seed germination. For the analysis, seeds are obtained from a local market in Sector-Tumkur and sterilized by soaking them in a HgCl2 solution for 10 min. To remove any residual HgCl2, the seeds are subsequently rinsed with doubly distilled water for 15 mins. The following Petri dish experiment is carried out to monitor seedling emergence and assess various growth parameters with different concentrations of TiO2 (Fig. 9 a). Seedling growth is monitored after 24 h. The germination sheet is then positioned in a tray (Fig. 9b), ensuring it is adequately sized to hold the seeds and facilitate water absorption. The sheet is dampened with distilled water to achieve a moist, but not overly saturated, condition. Using tweezers, the seeds are meticulously positioned on the damp germination sheet. The seeds are spaced evenly to prevent overcrowding and promote optimal growth. Using a spray bottle, gently mist the TiO2 NPs solution over both the seeds and the germination sheet, ensuring an even coating of the NPs solution on the seeds. A control system is also established to assess standard parameters [40].

Fig. 9(c & d) displays photographs of the germinated seeds after 5 and 10 days, respectively. The biocompatibility of the prepared TiO2, due to its small size and smooth morphology, has led to minimal damage to the cell walls and organelles of the seeds examined. Additionally, the characteristics of the seeds themselves have significantly influenced their germination ability in the presence of TiO2. For instance, mature dried seeds typically exhibit a greater capacity for water absorption, which promotes cellular metabolism and growth rate. Additionally, the emergence of rootlets in seeds is influenced by the types of micro nuclei present in the seeds. Several parameters are assessed to evaluate the impact of TiO2 on seed germination, including average root length, coleoptile length, germination percentage, Vigor Index I, germination index, percentage inhibition of growth and Seed Vigor II.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of average root and shoot length, diameter
ce:section.ce:para: The germination capacity of the selected seeds is further assessed by measuring the root and shoot lengths of the sprouted seeds (Fig. 10 a). The behavior of rootlet emergence is influenced by the micronuclei present in the seeds, which is affected by the non-toxic carboxyl groups on the surface of the prepared TiO2. Across 5 to 10 days of exposure, the root and shoot lengths of horse gram seeds exhibited a linear increase up to a TiO2 concentration level of 160 mg/mL [109]. This is regarded as the most significant result of the study. Additionally, compared to the control group, the 80 mg/mL TiO2 treatment enhanced the diameters of the shoots and roots by 58 % and 61 %, respectively (Fig. 10b). Fig. 10c shows the fresh biomass of the shoot and root. It is clear that the group treated with 80 mg/mL of TiO2 solution exhibited a higher biomass compared to the control group [110]. Furthermore, compared to the control, fresh biomass increased by 29 % for shoots and 37 % for roots after seed priming with the 80 mg/mL TiO2 solution. Additionally, dry biomass increased by 22 % for roots and 34 % for shoots (Fig. 10d). The measurements are provided in Table 5 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of germination index, seed germination and percentage inhibition growth
ce:section.ce:para: The benefits of the proposed method and its potential for enhancing agricultural and environmental applications have been demonstrated through the assessment of seed germination toxicity in the presence of the prepared TiO2 solution. The increased Germination Energy indicates that the synthesized TiO2 has significantly enhanced the seeds ability to germinate more uniformly and rapidly. During germination, seeds experience increased respiration and metabolic activation to convert food into energy. As TiO2 concentrations reached the optimal level, Fig. 11 a illustrates how the germination energy of horse gram seeds rose from 20 to 160 mg/mL of TiO2 and how the average time required for germination decreased. After ten days of harvesting, the germination ability remained stable across all TiO2 concentrations. Further investigation into the enhanced productivity of plants grown under optimal conditions showed that the germination index of seedlings increased linearly with higher TiO2 concentrations, as illustrated in Fig. 11b. Furthermore, the selected TiO2 is observed not to interfere with seed aquaporins or transmembrane water channels, which enhance and facilitate water permeability. As a result, the TiO2 solution is necessary to increase the rate of plant germination. The percentage inhibition of growth (PIG) values are found to be minimal at all concentrations, indicating that the TiO2 synthesized is both biocompatible and non-toxic when used with horse gram seeds. As the concentration of TiO2 increased from 20 to 160 mg/mL, the Percentage Inhibition of Growth (PIG) values decreased (Fig. 11c), highlighting the positive impact of TiO2 on plant growth. The results indicate that the low PIG values are associated with improved germination ability and a higher germination index, which aligns with the previously obtained findings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Vigor index I and seed Vigor II
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-proliferative activity of seeds has been significantly impacted by the peculiar qualities of TiO2, particularly impacting the Vigor Index values [111]. In addition to evaluating germination and viability, seed Vigor is a key quality measure that offers insights into seed performance. The average seedling length and germination rate are the primary factors influencing the Vigor Index I. Due to the generally consistent germination rate across all conditions, variations in average root length significantly impact the Vigor Index of the selected seeds. The average root length peaked at 80 mg/mL of TiO2, which yielded the greatest Vigor Index-I value (Fig. 11d). Given that seed Vigor is a crucial aspect of seed quality, we calculated the Seed Vigor II index (Fig. 11e). The two main components of this measure are the weight of the dried seedlings and their capacity to germinate. It can be concluded that the weight of the dry seedlings had the largest influence on the Seed Vigor II score, as the germination ability stayed consistent in each case. Throughout the germination investigation, the average weight of the seeds changed according to the TiO2 concentration. Consequently, a higher weight of seedlings is correlated with a stronger Seed Vigor index. The results indicate that both Seed Vigor II and Vigor Index I are optimized at 80 mg/mL of TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chlorophyll content and water uptake activity of horse gram seeds
ce:section.ce:para: Plant development is significantly influenced by the amount of chlorophyll since it is inversely correlated with the rate of photosynthetic reaction, the total number of light-harvesting complexes and the components involved in electron transport [112]. Plants of different kinds can have their leaves penetrated by TiO2, which also improve the thylakoid membrane structure. By raising the chlorophyll content, this enhancement encourages the growth of healthier, more mature plants [113]. In this study, horse gram seeds are exposed to normal light for ten days before the amount of chlorophyll is assessed. In comparison to the control group, the findings showed that the chlorophyll content peaked at mg/mL 80 concentration of TiO2 (Fig. 11f), suggesting greater photosynthetic activity because the TiO2 surface has more adsorption sites. The chlorophyll content is somewhat lower at 160 mg/mL and much lower than 80 mg/mL, but it is still greater than in the control group, indicating that the created system functions well at all tested doses (Table 6 ). Reduced levels of chlorophyll are associated with altered seed development and metabolism, which can impact horse gram seed germination and photosynthesis. The marginal decline in the germination index noted at 160 mg/mL concentration could be explained by the overabundance of TiO2 clogging seed pores, which hinders water absorption and thus germination processes [34]. The seeds that had sprouted had the maximum chlorophyll concentration (80 mg/mL), which is followed by the 20 mg/mL, 40 mg/mL, 60 PPM, 160 PPM and control.

In summary, we have demonstrated a straightforward, eco-friendly, and cost-effective method for synthesizing TiO2 NPs through a solution combustion technique using ESs. The TiO2 NPs are thoroughly characterized using XRD, XPS, FT-IR, UV–Vis spectroscopy, FE-SEM, and TEM techniques. Notably, the TiO2 NPs are effectively used for visualizing LFP on various substrates, including leaves, aluminum foil, and glass slides. The TiO2 NPs exhibit excellent display functionality and clearly defined ridge characteristics, demonstrating high sensitivity for LFP detection. Integrating YOLOvx8 into FP analysis workflows not only improves the accuracy of FP recognition systems but also significantly speeds up processing, making it ideal for high-throughput applications. Furthermore, our deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) accurately identifies bifurcations, ridge endings, loops, and scars. Notably, TiO2 demonstrates concurrent UV absorption capabilities. The PVA@TiO2 (16 wt%) film offered complete shielding with 90.11 % for UV-A, 92.82 % for UV-B, and 92.72 % for UV-C. These eco-friendly, flexible, thermally stable, and easily fabricated PVA@TiO2 (16 wt%) films show great potential for various applications, including UV-blocking sunglasses and window films. Furthermore, TiO2 NPs used as a priming agent at environmentally realistic concentrations ranging from 0 to 160 mg/mL have shown a significant improvement in the germination of horse gram seeds. Specifically, at the optimal concentration of TiO2 (80 mg/mL), germination rates increased by 100 %, and seedling growth improved by 54.5 % compared to the control. Additionally, the Vigor index showed a significant enhancement, rising by 61.5 %. These results highlight the potential of TiO2 NPs to improve seed performance, indicating their effectiveness as a nano-priming agent. Overall, the findings demonstrate that TiO2 NPs are promising for FP detection, UV shielding, and as a nano-priming agent in agricultural applications.
